VerbalDescription: Make the grid the same size. Copy the shapes, but put them in order from small to large. If they are horizontal small to large left to right. If they are vertical small to large top to bottom.
i follow the Task demonstration
The pattern was mirrored.
Copy everything to the new grid exactly the same. Now rearrange the object going from smallest to largest from top to bottom, left to right.

Same grid size and colours. Arrange the shapes so they are organised with the largest shapes to the smallest shapes. If the shapes are organised horizontally the largest shape should be at the right, if they are arranged vertically the largest shape should be at the bottom.
copy from input grid and re arrange the boxes in ascending order. From the smallest to the biggest with one square grid gap in between
i arranged it according to size while paying attention to the space in between.
Put the blocks in size order. If they are aligned horizontally, place them this way, if they are aligned vertically then place them that way instead.
You'll see several shapes on a black background. Keep all the shapes as is, and the grid size the same, but put the shapes in order from smallest to largest.
Use the same grid size as in the input. There will be one object in the input; in your answer, shift the object one grid square to the right.
Order the shapes from smallest to largest either horizontally or vertically depending on whether or not the grid is taller or wider.
Keep the colors and grid size the same as the input.  Rearrange the objects by size from smallest to largest.
Match the grid size, but reorder the blocks from smallest to biggest starting with smallest at the top.
Arrange squares in order from smallest to largest keeping all colors and directions the same

Use the same grid size. Rearrange the provided shapes, in the same colors, from largest to smallest, or smallest to largest, in the direction that they are in. If they are jumbled, move them so they are in increasing size from left to right or top to bottom.
Reorder the shapes in order from smallest to largest, starting from top to bottom, and keeping the shapes the same color as before.
Rearrange the shapes from smallest to largest, keep the same color
Use the same grid size as in the input. There will be one object in the input; in your answer, shift the object one grid square to the right.
Basically transpose the picture. Duplicate everything in reverse in a horizontal manner.
Same size grid and same colours.  Reverse the squares on the same side as they are currently closer to.

Grid size, height and width should be the same as the grid of the test input. Rearrange the different coloured grid shapes from their position according to their size from smallest to biggest. Grid colours remain thesame as the colour of the test input

Resize the image to 4 x 10. The squares need to be flipped around, remaining in size order. The biggest red block needs moved onto the right hand side, with the one in the middle remainin in the middle - one space along from the biggest red block. Then leave another space and put the smallest block on the left.
Reset grid to same size as input. starting in the top left corner, recreate the objects in ascending order of size, in the same direction as the are presented in the input.
Keep grid same size as the input. Keep same number of objects as the input, but rearrange them from smallest to biggest keeping all dimensions the same. Keep all colors the same.

Output should have a height of one and a width of however many colors are in the blocks, ignoring the black outer frame. Starting on the left place the outermost color and going right placing the next color until you reach the center color.
Resize the grid to only a horizontal rectangle made up of the colors of the original shape, from outer to inner color going left to right. Disregard black.

Find the amount of each color. Create a 1 by how many colors there are grid and place them in order of which one has the most amount of colored blocks. If there are 3 colors if would be a 1 by 3 grid.

Resize the grid to have a height of 1 and a width that is equal to the total number of colors present in the input. Then color each tile of the output to match a different color according to how they appear in the input grid, from left to right.

The rule is to count the number of colors in the original grid will equate to the length of the grid. The height of the grid is 1. The color layout will be based on the outermost color going to the innermost, and that will be from left to right in the new grid.

Resize the grid so that it has 1 height, but a width of the number of unique colours in the input grid. From left to right, change each block to the colours in the input shape, going from outside to inside the object.
Create a grid with a height of one. Fill in each square with the colour that appears first from the left in the input, and stop when there are no more new colours.

Change the grid size to 1x3. Based on the task demonstration, there is a pattern. Only the colors of the objects are highlighted. The colors shown are red, yellow, and purple. In your answer, fill in the three spaces with each of the different colors.
The grid should be resized to 1 square wide be 6 squares long. The shaded squares should match the order of the different colors in your shape. Keep the order of the colors to match the figure.

The input will have shell-objects, nesting objects in a layered way. The output should have a grid size of height 1, and width equal to the number of layers. In order, left-to-right, fill the squares in the colours of the layers, from the outside going inwards.
The solution is to make a row of colours used in the shape going from left to right, this does not include using black. Resize the grid to (1x6) as there are 6 colours used.

Look at the shape presented on the input grid. There should be x unique colours (where x is the actual number of unique colours). For example, if there were red, green and yellow, there would be three unique colours. Resize the output grid to have a height of 1 and a width of x (the number of unique colours), Then, change each 1x1 square (working from left to right) to each individual unique colour in the order that they appear on the input grid (working from left to right).

Make a grid that is one square high by as many squares wide as different colours are represented on the original picture. Colour these squares the same colours as are in the picture. For the order left to right, first take the colour on the outside of the concentric squares and work inwards.
Change the grid to 1 row and fill it with the colours from the pattern from outside to inside

Count the individual colors of the shape displayed in the test input grid. Change the width of the grid size to your final count leaving the height as 1. In the order from the outermost color to the innermost color fill in the grid by each color from left to right.
by quick decision making.

Ignore the black border surrounding the boxes. In center, you will see colored boxes inside each other. Count the number of colored boxes there are. Use grid size height 1, width = number of colored boxes. Now, from left to right side, each square is filled in as the color of each colored box from outside box to inside box. If there are 3 colored boxes colors A, B, C from outside to inside, then from left to right fill each square as colors A, B, C.

Find the main shape and count how many colours are in the middle (not including black).  Size your grid to 1x number of colours.  You must then put the colours in order starting from outside to in, going across in a line.

There will be many different colours in the input. Make sure your grid is only the height of one square and the width of each of the colours you can see. Colour the squares in exactly the order of the different colours you can see going across. Do not repeat the same colour again.

The grid should be 1 block (height) x width matching the amount of colours used in the demonstration. Fill the grid from left to right with the colours used in the demonstration, starting from the one used on the outside of the shape and finishing with the colour on the inside.

There will be a shape with coloured layers surrounded by black. Ignore the black. 

The solution is a line one block high. The leftmost block is the colour of the outer layer. The next block is the colour of the next layer. This continues until the rightmost block which is the colour of the centre of the shape.

Make your grid 1 height and depending on how many colours you shape has without including the black background make the width how many colours you have. Then going from left to right make 1 square of each colour going from the outer colour on the left to the middle colour on the right.
Use the same grid size as in the input. Rotate all objects to vertical, then arrange in ascending order starting from left side of grid.
Any line or single grid anywhere on the it is rearranged in ascending order vertically starting from the very left of the graph. Size and color is mainted verbatim.
Rearrange the lines vertically starting at the bottom left corner from smallest to tallest.

Keep the input grid size. Create an ascending staircase starting from the bottom left square. Use the number of squares in each input shape to guide how tall each step should be going from the smallest number to largest.
Resize the grid to 10 by 10. Then from the blue shapes given  make make horizontal, and from shorted to tallest, make a staircase on the bottom left.
Starting from the bottom left corner, align the solid colored lines vertically from shortest to tallest to create a staircase

Resize the grid to 10x10. Looking at the blocks, there are different sizes. Start with the smallest which is one and put it in the furthest bottom left position. Put the second smallest next to it and then the third and fourth. It should create a mountain effect, going from smallest on the left to tallest on the right.
Grid same, shapes moved to left corner with shortest on the left and tallest on the right, no gap between each.
Start at the bottom left of the grid. Start with the smallest feature. Set it vertical. Enter it at the bottom left. Repeat with all of the features.

Use the same grid size.  There will be multiple objects in the input.  Orientate all object vertically.  In the lower left corner of the grid arrange each object from smallest to biggest; each in it's own column.  Keep the color of the objects the same.
make the grid the same size as the original. Now order the colored lines, starting from the bottom left, in height order. going left to right. Make sure the lines are the same colour as the original.

The puzzle consists of lines all of the same colour placed randomly. Rearrange the lines vertically and place them in order of shortest to longest with the shortest at the the bottom left corner, and the next shortest immediately to the right.
Put the buildings in order of the number of windows they have.  I.e.building with one window first, then two windows, 3 windows etc.
COLOR GRID INTERCHANGE

Use the same grid size as the test grid size. Remake the objects in the test grid except swap the locations of the objects so that the one with the least amount of squares of its minority color pattern is on the left and the object with the most amount of squares on its minority color pattern is on the right, going in ascending order from left to right.
The image is mirrored.

Make a duplicate of the shape. Organize the columns by least to greatest, judging by the amount of squares that are a different color than the column. Do not change the location or colors of the squares in the column, just reorganize them.
Just swap the positions. Left one moves to the right, right one moves to the left.
Shift the shapes to the left, looping back around after the third shape. So whichever is in first moves to second, second to third, third to first.

Each object represents a number designated by the number of squares on each object that are a different color from the base. If the base color is orange for example (determined by the majority of squares being orange,) but it has 2 pink squares on it, its value is two. If it has 3 its value is 3, and so on. Recreate the grid and re-arrange the objects according to their assigned value from least to greatest.

The rule is to recreate the grid as seen on the input grid. However to reorganise each rectangle depending on the amount of different coloured blocks in each. Put the rectangle with the least amount of different coloured blocks to the left and work up as you move right,

Copy test input. Each object is dotted with a different colour in different amounts of dots. Rearrange the objects so they are in order of ascending amounts of dots starting with the lowest on the left and increasing in number of dots to the right.

reproduce the same size grid as the input. Count the input objects from the bottom beginning with the one with the lowest different colored squares. Reproduce the same shapes and patterns as the input but line them up, left to right, in the order corresponding to the order of the different colored squares, bottom to top.

Keep the same grid size and black background. Replicate the shape positions but change the order of the colour based on the position of the blocks in the reference image. The higher the shaded block, the further down the line it goes.

There are four vertical objects in the input box. Each are different colors–each is mainly one color, but with another marking on them in a different color. The order of the objects will change. Look at the markings of the objects. The one with the lowest marking will become the leftmost object. The next highest will be directly to its right, the next highest will be on that one's right, and so on. The one with the highest marking will be the rightmost object. Then remove the markings, making its object entirely its main color.

Copy the size of the grid on the input. There will be 4 shapes horizontally with a line/dot at different heights within the shapes. If this marker is higher up in the shape, that colour will be at the rightmost position, the next high up one will be second from right and so on
Find the lowest missing squares, then put the buildings in order by using the buildings with the lowest missing squares otherwise.  Copy across to keep the size.

See the coloured blocks on the grid and observe the space where the colour is broken. With the same sized grid, re-order the positions of the colours with the colour with the lowest place broken first, to the highest place broken last. In your solution, the blocks should be solid in colour.

Use exact grid size as original input grid. In demonstration, determine which "unique feature" (could be different color grid square, could be missing grid square, could be additional grid squares of a different color attached to object) on each object is higher or lower than the others. The object with the lowest unique feature will be in your solution first (reading the grid left to right). Keep exact shape, dimensions, and color as original, but change the location of the object so the object with the lowest unique feature in the demonstration is the same as the object that appears first in your submission. No object in your solution keeps its unique feature. Only the object itself, free of any unique feature, will be in your solution. The second object in your solution (L-R) will be the object with the second-lowest unique feature, and continue until all objects are represented in your solution.
the green of number different change in color of  yellow of number order.

Same grid size as input. Exactly copy the 4 sections of yellow dots separated by green vertical lines. Except, sort the figures by number of dots. From left to right, the sections should be: the 1 yellow dot figure, the 2 yellow dots figure, the 3 yellow dots figure, then the many yellow dots figure.

make the grid size the same. Now copy the green lines the same. This divides the quid into 4 cells. In the first cell on the left copy the pattern with the fewest yellow dots. The second cell, the second the pattern with the second fewest yellow dots. Third cell, third fewest. Forth cell the pattern with the most yellow dots.
Apply the ascending number of the box. For example, from 3,1,2,5 to 1,2,3,5. The smallest number of yellow grid should always be on the left and the biggest number should be on the right.

Recreate the grid, including the green lines. Then reorder the boxes in-between by the number of yellow squares, starting with the lowest number of yellow squares on the left and ending with the highest on the right.

The green lines separate 4 black boxes. 
Count the yellow squares in each box and re-order the boxes so that they are in numerical order based on the amount of yellow squares in each one. The re-ordered boxes need the yellow squares to be in the same place. 
(I'm fairly certain the second example is wrong. I've also already had this task)
Make the grid size 6 by 21. Then insert the vertical green lines in the same spots. Copy the same placements of each of the four yellow blocks in order from least to most.
Treat each block between the green lines as a cell, and order the cells based on the number of blocks inside the cell, keeping color and size.

This task has the green lines of squares act as 'barriers' to the yellow object 'sequences'. They are then sorted left to right in order of smallest number of yellow objects to highest number of yellow objects. To solve, copy from input to have the correct grid layout, then fill all the yellow object sequences with black squares. Then sort the yellow sequences from lowest number of yellow squares in the first black space and ending with the yellow sequence with the most yellow squares in the fourth black space.

Copy the exact same grid and layout of the original, in between the borders, order each section so the dots inside the boundary are in order e.g. if the original has dots in the other 2,4,3,1, change the shapes in between the boundaries so there are 1,2,3,4 in each boundary. The shape should match the original for each one

Copy the exact same grid and layout (green separators), in each cell, copy the shape of the yellow dots in the order of the amount of them. e.g. in the original if the amount of dots were 2,3,4,1, you would order them in 1,2,3,4, but keeping the same pattern as the original
take the number of yellow dots and put them in order taking the green as separate boxes, keep the pattern and colours the same.
Copy the same size grid and then place the yellow squares in numerical order 1 - 4 from left to right with each matching yellow block in the same position as they were originally
Put the color blocks in order of size starting from the bottom (largest) to top (smallest). Start from the bottom-most line, and maintain same color/block number combinations.

Keep the grid size the same. Rearrange the objects (grid patterns that have the same color) from smallest on top, to the largest on bottom. The placement starts in the lower left corner and moves up from there.
Keep the grid the same size. Sort the objects by size so the smallest is on top and the largest is on the bottom.
Sort the shapes so the smallest is at the top and they get larger as you go downwards. Kepp the sizes and colours of the shapes the same as the original.
make a 17x10 grid and then put the shapes across in order of size( so the 1 block is first then it was the 2 block then the next largest then the big green)

Copy the input grid so that it matches in the output grid. This task sort the different colored objects into size order, from smallest at the top to largest at bottom. Sort the objects in this order, making sure they are the exact same hieght and width as the input
follow the instruction.
THE EXAMPLE OF THE GRID IS VERY USEFUL TO TAKE A DECISION.
Copy grid. leave same number of blank rows on top. Arrange design from smallest to largest

Copy the same grid size. 
There will be 4 stacked shapes of different colours. 
Keeping the same shape colours and sizes, re-stack the shapes from largest to smallest from the bottom upwards.

Set grid size to 20 by 14. Place the largest object in the bottom left corner of the grid. Then, put the second largest object on top of the largest object as far to the left on the grid as possible. Then, put the third largest object on top of the second largest object as far to the left on the grid as possible. Finally, take the smallest object and put it on top of the third largest object as far to the left on the grid as possible.

There will be shapes of different sizes stacked. Keep the grid size the same from the input, but reorganise the shapes so that they are in size order, with the largest on the bottom working upwards to smallest. Keep the colours and dimensions the same.
Copy the grid size and keep the same colours from the original but re-arrange the patterns in ascending order, starting with the largest at the bottom and smallest at the top.
i choose the right Height and width, the arranged the blocks accordingly as on the image on the left . i used the EDIT command
Reorder the shapes by size. Smallest should be at the top and largest on the bottom
Keep grid and colors the same. Take the colored shapes and stack them on top of one another with the largest on the bottom and getting smaller as you go up .
Keeping the grid size the same, Copy the shapes over but sort them in size from largest at the bottom to smallest at the top.
flip it up
Use the same grid size as in the input. There will be one object in the input; in your answer, shift the object one grid square to the right.

Use the same grid size as the test grid size. Replicate the grey line in the center of the test grid. Remake each side of the test grid with the yellow square patterns but the side with more yellow squares should be on the right side of the grey line, while the side with less squares should be on the left side of the grey line.

Keep the grid size the same and the gray line dividing the grid into two sections. Count the number of yellow squares in each black section of the grid. Arrange it so the section section with the fewest squares is on the left and the most squares is on the right. If it is already arranged like this, just submit an exact copy of the original grid.

Copy the input grid size as well as the colored line of squares down the middle column. then look at each side of that column as a panel. You need to switch them, reproducing the left panel on the right and vice versa. Keep the original orientation of the objects within each side panel.

Resize the grid to the same as the original. Mark the grey central column. If there are more shapes on the right side of the grey column, replicate the original exactly. If there are more shapes on the left side of the grey column, swap the two halves either side of the grey column around, do not mirror. Keep the squares the same colurs as the originals.

Resize your grid to the test input size. Fill the exact center, horizontal line with the same color as the test input. There are patterns of squares on either side of the line. Replicate these patterns, with the same color, in your grid on the opposite side of the line they were originally on.

Use the same grid size and colours.  Count the yellow 'windows'.  The order will depend on the amount of windows and the lowest number of windows go first.  So if the task has 3 windows and 2 windows, the new order will then revert to 2 windows then 3 windows.

Replicate the grid size mine was Hight x 6 Width x 21

Place a grey line 1 x cell down the grid in the centre.

now count the coloured squares either side of the grey line

the highest amount one any given side gets replicated on the far right of the grey line

the lesser amount of coloured squares gets replicated to the far left of the grey square.
Copy grid size from input. Shapes are divided into two groups by grey line. The size and color of the shapes do not change. If there are more shapes on the left, swap each side.
Use the same size grid as input. Also do the objects in the exact same place as input and the same colour. Do not change anything just copy it

Use the same grid size as in the input. Based on the task demonstrations, there is a pattern that follows: swap grids, don't swap, swap grids. If you continue the pattern, the choice is to not swap the positions of the grids. In your answer, copy the grids as they are shown and make no changes.

Copy the same grid size as the input. If, in the input, there are more yellow squares on the left of the gray line, reverse the image and redraw it so there are more yellow squares on the right (as if the two sides have swapped places). If there are already less yellow squares on the left side of the gray line than on the right in the input image, copy the image as is and do not change it.

First, copy the original grid and all its elements as is. Second, count the number of distinct objects on each side of the divider in the middle. If the number of objects on the left side of the divider is lesser than the amount on the right, then the solution is identical to the original. If the amount on the left is greater than the amount on the right, simply flip the sides so that the lesser objects are on the left. If flipping like this, ensure that every object is in exactly the same place and color as it originally was, just on the other side of the divider.

Same grid size, same colours.  The order of the 2 blocks will depend on the number of yellow 'windows'.  If there is one block with 3 windows and one with 2, the lowest will go first, so 2 windows then 3.
Same grid size with grey column, shapes to the left of grey column go to right hand side and vice versa. Same colours.

The rule is that you are going to take 1 of each color and line them up horizontally. Start by resetting the grid to a height of 1 and width of 5 since there are 5 colors. Select only the first box on the left. Then click the teal color and click the left box again. Now click the second box (next to teal) and choose the orange color. Now click the second box to make it orange. Now click the middle box and choose the green color. Then click the middle box to make it green. Now choose the 4th box. Click the dark blue color at the bottom and click the box again to make it dark blue. Choose the fifth box on the right side and choose the gray color. Click the fifth box and make it gray.

First create a grid that is 1 square high.  The width should equal the number of colors.  The first color on the left should be the color that is one the top.  The second color should be the color underneath the top color.  Repeat until the grid is filled color.  The vertical colors top to bottom should match the horizontal colors left to right.
Make the grid a square for each color and color fill each block with a color from the grid starting with the top color going down to the bottom color.

the rule for this one was to create a single height line which each tile representing the order of colours present in each vertical tower from top to bottom. i.e. the task demonstration tower goes magenta, yellow, green, peach from top to bottom, therefore the solution is a single row of magenta, yellow, green and then peach.

Imagine flipping the shape to the left & selecting 1 of each colour out of the different shades. KEEP THE COLOURS IN THE SAME ORDER. Apply this to the grid & ensure these coloured cells fill the entire grid, (the grid will need to be made smaller)

The colors from top to bottom should be represented in the final answer which will be of a single line of squares from left to right. If five colored shapes then final answer is five squares. Color of top shape should be color of first square which is also the leftmost square in the line of final answer. Color of bottom shape should be color of last square in the line of squares. no black squares in final answer.

resize the grid to 1 x 4. 
the old grid should have a vertical line of shapes. 
list the colors of each shape from top to bottom
fill in your 1 x 4 grid from left to right using the same order of colors as the top to bottom of the old grid.
create a 4 wide x 1 high grid and then make the colours of the blocks match those of the input using top to bottom and left to right
use every color once but in horizontal manner

Resize the grid to be 1 square high, and as many squares wide as there are different colours used in the input. Then make each grid space in the new grid the colours in descending height order from the input

You should see four continuous objects of different colours in the input grid. You should resize the grid to be one block tall and four blocks wide. You should then colour the four squares of your output grid from the input grid - the first square (from left to right) should be the top colour, the second square should be the second-highest colour, the third square should be the third-highest colour, and the fourth square should be the bottom colour.

Make the grid only 1 size in height and then as wide as you have blocks. After that starting from the top put 1 of each color from left to right. Colors can only repeat if they've had a different color between them.

make a grid that is 1 tall. Make the width equal to the number of color changes in the original pattern, excluding black. color the squares in the new grid with one square for each color in order of the vertical color changes of the original shape
Take one grid of each color in line and place them vertically and resize to that shape

You will see a vertical shape consisting of a number of colours.
The solution is to represent the colours horizontally in a grid 1 block high.
For example, you could have a vertical shape of 4 pink blocks 4 yellow blocks 4 green block and 4 red blocks. Pink being the top and red being the bottom.
For this solution we are ignoring the shape or how many blocks there are and focusing on the colours. 
In this example you should create a grid that is 1 block high and 4 blocks wide, consisting of 1 pink 1 yellow 1 green and 1 red going from left to right.

You will have a vertical shape made of different coloured areas. For this solution only the order of the colours is important, not the size or shape of the areas.
For the solution you will represent each coloured area using one block. Your solution should be in a grid 1 block high.
So if you had a shape that consisted of the colours pink, yellow, green and red in descending order. Your solution should be a 1x4 grid of 1 pink block, 1 yellow block, 1 green block and 1 red block going from left to right.

There will be a vertical stack of shaded cells on your input. This could either be 1 cell wide or it could be more, but the vertical shape will be made up of different colours. Equally the tower could be 1 cell tall for each colour, or more, but this does not make a difference. The solution will be to make a 1 cell tall shape, and then as many cells wide as there are different 'sections' to your tower. Note, if one colour comes up twice in your tower then this counts as a separate section. In your solution you need to go from left to right, shading each cell the colour of the sections of your tower, starting from top to bottom.

so you leave however many blocks dark that are at the top. then you take the largest number set of blocks after the top, then the second largest, then the third largest, then the smallest last, all seperated by 1 black row. so it should be however many black at the top left alone, then 5, black row, 4, black row, 2, black row, 1

Change the grid size to the same size as the input. There will be three objects in the input. You need to arrange the shapes in order from the bottom up with black lines in between each shape. The bottom will be the shortest height object, then the next tallest object and so forth until all shapes are stacked from shortest to tallest going up. Keep all the objects shapes and colors the same.
copy the grid size. proceeding from bottom. copy the smallest shape to the original to the bottom of the new grid. skip a black line and copy the next bigger size. repeat with all shapes
Use the same size grid.  The space in between the green rows were added in numerical order.  Use the same amount of black blocks at the top.

Take the number of grids in each of the shapes and order them from top to bottom in order of the number of grids, meaning 1 row of grids, 4, 2 and 5, will be order like 5, 4, 2, and 1. Keep the colors the same.

Use the same size grid as the original. There are four objects; all green, and varying heights. The 4x3 green object remains in the same position as in the original. The 3x3 green object is sifted up by five grid squares. The 2x3 green object is shifted down by four grid squares. The 1x3 green block is shifted down by four grid squares. Each green block in the original remains the same colour in your answer.
Duplicate the image. Keep the top shape the same. Organize the lower shapes from largest to smallest, keeping their same color and dimensions and leaving a blank bar in between.
Same grid size, same shapes and colours, just copy it across.
Keep everything the same in terms of grid, colours, objects etc. Then re-order the blocks with the largest at the top, smallest at the bottom, making sure to keep a line of black blocks between them.
make a 18x3 grid and just copy it the exact same

Use the same grid size as in the input. Select copy from input. Since the pattern of the demonstration is to organize the objects from greatest to least, there is no need to change your answer. Submit.
